method: Live-state test of cascade_monitor.py on current swarm (S436). Ran tool 6 times (3 modes x 2 repetitions) to measure: (a) which layers are detected as failing, (b) whether cascades are detected, (c) consistency across runs, (d) execution time, (e) bugs and limitations. Compared output against ground truth from knowledge_state.py, eval_sufficiency.py, and maintenance.py direct invocations.

pre_registration.expect: cascade_monitor.py detects >=2 active cascades in current state, consistent with L-1008 finding of 100% cascade rate (50/50 sessions had >=1 active cascade)
pre_registration.success_criterion: Tool runs without error, detects cascades, output is actionable
pre_registration.falsification: If tool reports 0 cascades, either tool is broken or L-1008 100% rate was wrong, or the definition of cascade (>=2 adjacent layers failing) is too strict for current state

data.consistency: 5/6 runs fully consistent (first run had K layer error due to concurrent code update mid-test)

T.assessment: CORRECTLY FIRING — real stale refs exist, though 1 of 6 files is self-referential (cascade_monitor.py references S189/S393 in retroactive_assessment doc comments)

Q.assessment: CORRECTLY OK — DUE count fluctuates 2-4 due to concurrent sessions but is below threshold. FP proxy only matches literal keywords (hardcoded/false.positive/stale.*baseline) — crude but functional

K.assessment: CORRECTLY FIRING — BLIND-SPOT 16.4% exceeds 15% threshold. DECAYED 27.1% close to but below 30% threshold. Both rates are real per knowledge_state.py. Note: K layer reads from latest experiments/meta/knowledge-state-s*.json file (stale if no recent knowledge_state.py run). Current file is S436 vintage — fresh.

E.assessment: CORRECTLY OK — avg_lp=4.6 is well above threshold. E layer bug was fixed by concurrent session during this test (initially read default 2.0 due to missing avg_lp key; now reads correct field).

A.assessment: LIKELY FALSE NEGATIVE — A layer FP detection relies on string matching ['already','stale','resolved'] in orient.py notice lines. This is extremely crude. Orient.py output is formatted with '.' prefix but line parsing is fragile. With only 1 notice detected, the A layer is probably underfiring.

cascade_detection.explanation: T and K are both failing but NOT adjacent in the cascade model. T connects to Q,A. K connects to Q,E,A. For a cascade, an intermediary (Q, E, or A) would also need to be failing.

actual: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED. Tool runs successfully and detects 2 failing layers (T and K), but reports 0 active cascades. This contradicts pre-registration expectation of >=2 cascades. Three root causes: (1) T and K are not adjacent in the cascade adjacency model, so their co-failure does not constitute a cascade by the tool's definition. (2) L-1008's 100% cascade rate used a broader cascade definition (any multi-layer failure pattern) than cascade_monitor.py's stricter definition (>=2 ADJACENT layers failing). (3) The tool has a definitional gap: T(stale tools) and K(knowledge decay) ARE causally connected — stale tools produce stale knowledge — but the adjacency model lacks a T-K edge.
diff: Expected >=2 cascades; observed 0. The gap is definitional, not instrumental. The tool's layer checks work correctly (T and K are genuinely failing, Q/E/A are genuinely OK). The cascade detection algorithm works correctly per its adjacency model. The bug is in the adjacency model itself: it omits the T→K causal pathway (stale tools → stale knowledge). This is a model specification error, not a code bug.

bugs_found.description: E layer check originally defaulted to avg_lp=2.0 when key not found in eval_sufficiency.py JSON. The actual field is data.goals.Increase.avg_lp_per_session, not data.avg_lp. Fixed by concurrent session during this test.

T layer self-referential detection: cascade_monitor.py's own retroactive_assessment function contains S189 and S393 string literals that match the stale-baseline regex. Tool inflates its own T layer count by 1 file (3 refs).

Adjacency model missing T→K edge. Stale tools (T) cause stale knowledge (K) — this is the C1/C4 cascade mechanism. Without this edge, the most common cascade pattern (stale baseline → knowledge decay) goes undetected as a cascade even when both layers are failing.

Q layer FP proxy uses only 3 keyword matches (hardcoded|false.positive|stale.*baseline). This is too narrow — maintenance DUE items that are false positives rarely contain these exact strings. FP rate is always 0% in practice, making the Q→failing path via fp_rate effectively dead code.

A layer FP detection uses string matching on ['already','stale','resolved'] in orient.py notice lines. Orient.py output format is variable and these keywords are too specific. Only 1 notice line detected across all runs, suggesting massive underfiring.

K layer reads latest knowledge-state-s*.json by filename sort. If a concurrent session writes a newer file during cascade_monitor execution, the K layer could read a partially-written file. No crash observed but race condition exists.

limitations: Execution time 38-54s is too slow for real-time monitoring; would need to run as periodic, not inline
Q layer fp_rate effectively dead (always 0%) — needs better FP detection heuristic
A layer FP detection is nearly nonfunctional (crude keyword match)
Adjacency model is hardcoded and missing at least 1 edge (T→K)
Retroactive assessment is static (hardcoded C1-C5 cases) — not driven by data
No temporal tracking: cannot measure onset lag in real time, only retroactively
Cascade detection is purely structural (adjacency check), not causal or temporal
E layer uses subprocess call to eval_sufficiency.py which is expensive (~15-20s)
lesson_candidate: L-CANDIDATE: cascade_monitor.py live test reveals definition-measurement gap. Tool correctly detects 2 failing layers (T, K) but reports 0 cascades because its adjacency model lacks T-K edge. L-1008 100% cascade rate used implicit broader definition (any multi-layer co-failure). Effect size: 100% rate → 0% rate depending on definition. Root cause: cascade adjacency model was designed from L-1007's 5 named patterns (C1-C5) which all flow through Q or A as intermediaries. T→K direct pathway (stale tools → stale knowledge) was omitted. Also: Q.fp_proxy and A.fp_proxy are effectively dead sensors (always 0). 3 of 5 layer checks (Q, E-before-fix, A) had detection bugs reducing effective layer coverage to 2/5 (T, K only). Implication: cross-layer monitoring requires live calibration against known-failing states before deployment, not just retroactive assessment against historical cases. Cites: L-1007, L-1008, P-300, L-601.
